participants.extracted: Two hundred ten children ages 4–11 years with confirmed contact sexual abuse were randomly assigned, along with 164 accompanying non-offending caregivers/parents, to one of four TF-CBT conditions (8 sessions with no trauma narrative, 8 sessions with trauma narrative, 16 sessions with no trauma narrative, or 16 sessions with trauma narrative). After excluding dropouts, 179 child–parent dyads/completers (children who attended at least 3 sessions) were included in the analyses. The child sample was 61% girls with a mean age of 7.7 years (SD 2.1); 65% were Caucasian, 14% African American, 7% Hispanic, and 14% other ethnic origins. Most caregivers were biological mothers (84%). Children were recruited from Pittsburgh, PA and Stratford, NJ, and all had at least five PTSD symptoms with confirmed CSA exposure.

interventions.extracted: Children with a history of contact sexual abuse and their non-offending parents were randomized to one of four trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy (TF-CBT) conditions: 8 sessions without a trauma narrative (8 No TN), 8 sessions with a trauma narrative (8 Yes TN), 16 sessions without a trauma narrative (16 No TN), or 16 sessions with a trauma narrative (16 Yes TN). TF-CBT included psychoeducation and parenting, relaxation, affective modulation, cognitive coping, in vivo exposure, conjoint parent-child sessions, and enhancing safety and future development. In the Yes TN conditions, children were actively guided to develop, expand, and process a detailed trauma narrative with the therapist and parent; these conditions included 3-4 narrative sessions in the 8-session arm and at least twice that number in the 16-session arm. In the No TN conditions, children and parents received the same core TF-CBT components but were not encouraged to discuss or write about the details of the abuse. Across all groups, sessions typically involved individual child and parent work, parenting skills training, and conjoint sessions, with the 16-session conditions providing more opportunities for repetition and review of psychoeducation and skills practice.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure child PTSD symptoms using the K-SADS Present and Lifetime Version at baseline and post-treatment follow-up points (8 weeks for the 8-session conditions and 16 weeks for the 16-session conditions), with symptom clusters including Reexperiencing, Avoidance, and Hypervigilance summed from child and parent interviews. Parent-reported outcomes included depression measured by the Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II), child externalizing and internalizing behavior problems measured by the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL), child sexualized behavior measured by the Child Sexual Behavior Inventory (CSBI), parental emotional distress related to the abuse measured by the Parent Emotional Reaction Questionnaire (PERQ), and parenting practices measured by the Parent Practices Questionnaire (PPQ), assessed at baseline and post-treatment follow-up. Child self-reported outcomes included depression measured by the Children’s Depression Inventory (CDI), abuse-related fear measured by the Fear Thermometer (Fear), global anxiety measured by the Multidimensional Anxiety Scale for Children (MASC), shame related to the abuse measured by the Shame Questionnaire (Shame), and body-safety/response skills measured by the What If Situations Test (WIST), assessed at baseline and post-treatment follow-up. The study also assessed all outcomes again at interim and later follow-up evaluations as part of the broader assessment schedule, including a 4-week evaluation and 6- and 12-month follow-ups, although the primary treatment comparison focused on the post-treatment time point corresponding to treatment completion.
